children.value: Introduce the concept of a 4-electrode measurement

Previously, we saw that soil can be physically characterised by its electrical resistivity. Here, we extend this concept to electrical resistivity tomography. Electrical Resistivity Tomography (ERT) is a near-surface geophysical method that images the spatial distribution of the
(or its inverse, electrical conductivity) of the subsurface. Unlike borehole or soil-core measurements — which are localised and destructive — ERT is
, and can be repeated over time to track
soil water content, salinity, clay content and temperature
Bridges the gap between sparse point sensors and large-scale remote sensing

Tomography refers to the spatial reconstruction of a physical property within a medium from indirect measurements. The term is also widely used in medicine — most famously in CT (Computed Tomography) scans.
The 4-electrode concept: apparent resistivity and geometric Factor

Why four electrodes? avoiding contact resistance

children.label: Why four electrodes? avoiding contact resistance

children.value: Using four electrodes separates the roles of
, which is crucial for eliminating the influence of
Contact resistance at these electrodes does not affect the voltage measurement because voltage is measured separately.
Measure the voltage drop in the subsurface.
because they are connected to a high-impedance voltmeter.
As a result, the voltage drop across their contact resistance is insignificant, and the measured voltage (Vₘₙ) reflects only the subsurface resistivity.
By separating these roles, the measurement is
, ensuring that ρₐ accurately represents the subsurface conditions.
is the resistivity value calculated from field measurements using four electrodes. It is called “apparent” because it represents an
of the subsurface volume influenced by the electrode configuration. The subsurface is rarely homogeneous, so ρₐ is an effective value that simplifies interpretation.
The apparent resistivity is calculated using the formula:

children.html: ρa=K⋅VMNIAB\rho_a = K \cdot \frac{V_{MN}}{I_{AB}}ρa​=K⋅IAB​VMN​​

children.value: ERT survey design and sequence strategy

children.label: ERT survey design and sequence strategy

children.value:
Choosing the right measurement sequence is fundamental: the electrode
configuration controls sensitivity, depth of investigation and resolution.

The optimal sequence, electrode spacing, and injection parameters are determined
through an

step: a plausible subsurface model is
assumed, synthetic data are computed, and array performance (resolution, signal
strength) is evaluated before deploying in the field
A standard ERT processing chain consists of the following steps:

children.html: R=ΔV/IR = \Delta V / IR=ΔV/I

children.value: – assign data uncertainties (needed for regularised inversion)

– finite-element or finite-difference mesh adapted to
topography and electrode positions

– iterative minimisation of a cost function to recover the
resistivity model that fits the data within its uncertainties

– independent or ratio/difference inversion to resolve
subsurface changes over time
) implements steps 2–6 in a single, user-friendly environment.

children.alt: Temporal dynamics of key soil properties following wildfire (after Dimech et al.). Shaded zones indicate the three intervention windows: emergency (red), early recovery (orange), and late recovery (green).

children.value: Temporal dynamics of key soil properties following wildfire (after Dimech et al.). Shaded zones indicate the three intervention windows: emergency (red), early recovery (orange), and late recovery (green).

Interpreting an inverted resistivity model requires combining geophysical
knowledge with site-specific information:

are compared to known lithological or soil
ranges (e.g., saturated clays: 1–10 Ω·m; dry sands: 1000+ Ω·m)

identify structural features (layer boundaries,
preferential flow paths, root zones)

(time-lapse) are converted to changes in water content
via petrophysical relationships, with explicit uncertainty propagation

(soil cores, TDR probes, sap-flow sensors)
is essential for unambiguous interpretation

Petrophysical relationships are site-specific and non-unique.  Always report
the uncertainty bounds on any moisture estimate derived from ERT data
